attributes.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with a two-day history of frequency, nocturia, and burning on micturition. This is the third episode of such symptoms he has had over the last two months and on this occasion his urine is dark brown. He thinks he might have noticed some bubbles, but no frothing in his urine. He has no other current health issues and is not taking any medications. About three months ago he had an episode of lower abdominal pain and fever which settled spontaneously after three days. The physical examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely underlying problem?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has pneumaturia. There are a number of possible explanations, including a urinary tract infection with a gas-producing organism. However, the usual and most common explanation is a colovesical fistula and these most often occur as a complication of diverticular disease.

Diverticulosis is typically an age-dependent disease; increasing from less than 20 percent at age 40 to 60 percent by age 60. Of these individuals, approximately 4% will develop acute diverticulitis. Chronic inflammation from diverticular disease or acute diverticulitis can result in the formation of a fistula between the colon and adjacent organs and tissue. Fistulas most commonly involve the bladder (i.e., a colovesicular fistula). The literature has cited that diverticulitis accounts for between 65-70% of colovesicular fistulas. Other contributors are cancer and long-standing Crohn's which account for 10-20% and 5-7%, respectively. Common clinical signs of a colovesicular fistula are pneumaturia (in up to 95% of cases), fecaluria, and suprapubic pain.

  • Bladder calculi may develop due to accumulation of minerals in the bladder. Stones may be asymptomatic or symptomatic (typically with urgency, pain, haematuria, dysuria, nausea, and vomiting). Most stones are composed of calcium oxalate and are related to dehydration or medication ingestion. This patient's history is not consistent with many of these features and the presence of pneumaturia makes another diagnosis more likely.

  • Unresolved E. coli urinary infection: Urinary infections can originate from the upper or lower urinary tract and present with features based on the site of infection. Typical features include dysuria, fever and flank pain paired with urgency and in some cases haematuria. Diagnosis is typically made with urinalysis with MC&S showing pus cells and gram negative bacteria. While this patient does have some features of an acute infection it is unlikely to be the cause of his pneumaturia. When pneumaturia is related to a primary urinary tract infection (UTI), the infection is usually long-standing. Whilst this patient could have an unresolved UTI, based on probabilities, the underlying problem is more likely to be a colovesical fistula.

  • While fistulas can occur in Crohn's disease they are not the most likely cause in this patient, particularly as he has no symptoms suggestive of inflammatory bowel disease. Such features would include fever, weight loss, and fatigue paired with a prolonged course of intestinal symptoms like non-bloody diarrhoea, abdominal pain, and malabsorption.

  • Carcinoma of the colon can cause fistulas (in 10-20% of cases) but this patient does not have any symptoms suggestive of a colorectal malignancy. These might include constitutional symptoms of tiredness, lassitude and weight loss and varied symptomology based on right or left-sided colon involvement (eg rectal bleeding, change in bowel habit).
learningPoints: Colovesical fistulas are most commonly a complication of diverticulitis, cancer, or Crohn's disease. Diverticulitis accounts for 65 to 79% of cases.
Diverticular disease is usually asymptomatic; patients may have constipation or nonspecific abdominal symptoms.
Symptomatic acute diverticulitis presents with the classic triad: fever, leukocytosis, and left lower quadrant pain.
references: Pemberton J. Colonic diverticulosis and diverticular disease: Epidemiology, risk factors, and pathogenesis [Internet]. Uptodate.com. 2021 [cited 25 July 2021]. Available from: <https://www.uptodate.com/contents/colonic-diverticulosis-and-diverticular-disease-epidemiology-risk-factors-and-pathogenesis>
Strickland M, Burnstein M, Cohen Z. Colovesical fistulas [Internet]. Uptodate.com. 2021 [cited 25 July 2021]. Available from: <https://www.uptodate.com/contents/colovesical-fistulas>
